econSignals.label: Fastest Growing Sector: ICT / Freelancing
econSignals.description: Bangladesh is the world's 2nd largest freelancing workforce. ICT freelancing grew at 14.6% annually 2018–2023, with Upwork and Fiverr among primary platforms.

econSignals.label: Light Manufacturing (non-RMG) Growth
econSignals.description: Footwear, leather goods, and jute products are growing segments with strong export demand. Offer more automation resilience than garments.

econSignals.label: Median Monthly Earnings — ICT / Freelancing
econSignals.description: Entry-level ICT/freelancing workers in Dhaka earn a median of ~BDT 28,000/month ($240 USD). Range: $120–$600 depending on skill level and platform.

econSignals.label: Median Monthly Earnings — RMG (Garments)
econSignals.description: Garment workers earn a median of ~BDT 13,500/month ($110). The NMW is $95/month (BDT 12,500). Highly routine task content makes this sector a medium-term automation risk.

econSignals.label: Returns to Upper Secondary Education (HSC)
econSignals.source: World Bank Human Capital Index / WDI
econSignals.description: HSC-level workers in Bangladesh earn 32% more than JSC-level workers on average. Polytechnic diploma adds a further 28% over HSC in manufacturing and construction.

econSignals.label: Youth NEET Rate (not in education, employment, or training)
econSignals.description: Nearly 1 in 3 Bangladeshi youth are NEET — disproportionately young women, particularly in rural areas. This represents 9+ million young people with unrecognised skills.

econSignals.label: Formal Employment Share
econSignals.description: Only 14% of Bangladesh's workforce is formally employed. The remaining 86% are in informal self-employment, casual labor, or unregistered work.
